coredata.dc:title: A novel 3D coordination polymer constructed by dual-ligand for highly sensitive detection of purine metabolite uric acid
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Xiao-Yang
Yang, Qi-Shan
Wang, Jia
Fu, Dong-Lei
Jiang, Dao-Kuan
coredata.dc:description: Uric acid (UA), as the final product of purine metabolism, exists in urine and serum, which plays an important role in human metabolism, immunity and other functions. The sensitive, efficient, and rapid detection of UA has far-reaching significance in clinical diagnosis and disease prevention. Herein, a novel coordination polymer constructed by dual-ligand was successfully prepared, which exhibited excellent thermal and water stability. The polymer was interlaced by coordination bonds and hydrogen bonds to form an infinitely extended three-dimensional framework, which showed a rare and novel topological structure. The complex selectively recognized UA through significant fluorescence quenching response in the presence of various interferences. The excellent detection sensitivity (the limited detection of 1.2 μM), outstanding anti-interference ability and remarkable recyclability marked the complex to be a promising sensor material towards UA. In addition, the detection mechanism of UA by the complex was investigated in detail by combining density functional theory (DFT) and a variety of other analytical methods.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Coordination polymer
Purine metabolite
Uric acid (UA)
Fluorescent sensor
Photoinduced electron transfer (PET)

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Inner Mongolia Natural Science Foundation (No. 2016ZD05 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Asymmetric unit of the complex with atom labeling; (b) The coordination modes of the dual-ligand and the coordination geometry of Eu3+ ion (Symmetry code: A: -x, 1-y, -z; B: 1-x, 1-y, -z); (c) The infinite 1D double-chains structure formed by coordination bonds along the crystallographic a-axis; (d) Various intermolecular hydrogen bond interactions (dotted lines) in different directions in the complex (dotted lines); (e) 2D layers were connected by O-H···O and N-H···O hydrogen bonding interactions in ab plane; (f) C-H···O and O-H···O hydrogen bonds of the complex in c-axis direction.
(a) The 3D supramolecular network of the complex formed by hydrogen bond interactions; (b) The complex with a novel topological grid structure.
(a) PXRD patterns of the complex immersed in different pH solutions for 24 h; (b) The thermal gravimetric (TGA) curve of the complex.
(a) The excitation and emission spectra of the complex in the solid state; (b) A schematic diagram of the energy transfer process between Eu3+ and ligands.

(a) Fluorescence behavior of the complex in different organic molecules; (b) The luminescence responses of the complex toward different concentrations of UA in aqueous solution; (c) Linear relationships between the fluorescence intensity (I/I0) and concentration of UA; (d) Fluorescence intensity of the complex after adding various interferents in the absence (blue) and presence (red) of UA.
Schematic representation of electronic structures for UA and Hntca.

head.ce:title: A novel 3D coordination polymer constructed by dual-ligand for highly sensitive detection of purine metabolite uric acid

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Inner Mongolia University of Science and Technology, Baotou 014000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Inner Mongolia University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Inner Mongolia University of Science and Technology, Baotou 014000, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Inner Mongolia University of Science and Technology, Baotou 014000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Inner Mongolia University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Uric acid (UA), as the final product of purine metabolism, exists in urine and serum, which plays an important role in human metabolism, immunity and other functions. The sensitive, efficient, and rapid detection of UA has far-reaching significance in clinical diagnosis and disease prevention. Herein, a novel coordination polymer constructed by dual-ligand was successfully prepared, which exhibited excellent thermal and water stability. The polymer was interlaced by coordination bonds and hydrogen bonds to form an infinitely extended three-dimensional framework, which showed a rare and novel topological structure. The complex selectively recognized UA through significant fluorescence quenching response in the presence of various interferences. The excellent detection sensitivity (the limited detection of 1.2 μM), outstanding anti-interference ability and remarkable recyclability marked the complex to be a promising sensor material towards UA. In addition, the detection mechanism of UA by the complex was investigated in detail by combining density functional theory (DFT) and a variety of other analytical methods.

ce:section.ce:para: Uric acid (UA) is the final product of purine metabolism and an important biomolecule in biological fluids such as serum and urine, which is mainly produced in the liver and excreted from the kidneys through urine [1–3]. The research indicated that the normal concentration of uric acid in human urine is 1.49 to 4.46 mM, and the normal value of uric acid in blood samples is 0.13 to 0.46 mM [4]. Abnormal UA levels are often a sign of gout, diabetes, hyperuricemia and cardiovascular diseases. Therefore, the content of UA in the human body is an important index to measure human health [5–7]. Up to date, various methods were developed for UA detection, including fluorescent methods [8,9], electrochemical analysis [10], capillary electrophoresis[11], colorimetry [12] and so on. Among them, the fluorescent method is favored for the low cost, convenience, fast response, and excellent repeatability [13–15]. The rapid breakthrough of fluorescence detection technology made great contributions to the development of medicine, life science, chemistry, material science and other fields [16,17].

Among all kinds of fluorescence sensors, lanthanide coordination polymer (Ln-CPs) with high sensitivity, short response time and remarkable anti-interference ability attracted much attention [18,19]. Organic ligands as “antenna molecules” effectively transfer the absorbed energy to the metal center, thus showing the unique optical properties of the lanthanide ions, such as large Stokes shift, high luminescence efficiency, long fluorescence lifetime and excellent luminescence stability [20,21]. Ln-CPs is widely used in the detection of metal ions, anions, toxic gases, nitro-explosives, and organic molecules, which is due to the novel topological structure, as well as great possibilities of structural modification and functionalization [22–24].

Inspired by the above ideas, herein, a novel luminescent coordination polymer was prepared by the reaction of pyromellitic acid (H4btec) and nicotinic acid (Hntca) with Eu(NO3)3·6H2O under solvothermal conditions. The complex exhibited the characteristic emission of Eu3+ (593, 613 and 692 nm) and a wide emission band of H4btec (500 nm). The triplet energy levels of the dual-ligand were calculated by using the B3LYP /6-31G* (d, p) functional in Gauss09D.01 Linux software. The energy level difference between the triplet energy level of H4btec and the 5D0 vibrational energy level of Eu3+ was too large to transfer the energy to Eu3+ effectively. In contrast, the vibrational energy levels of Hntca and Eu3+ were matched, indicating that Hntca can be used as “antenna molecule” to sensitize the luminescence of the earth ion. Interestingly, the addition of UA significantly quenched the characteristic fluorescence of Eu3+, and the fluorescence intensity showed a good linear relationship with UA concentration. Even in the presence of other potential interferences, UA was quickly, sensitively, and selectively recognized. More importantly, the complex showed outstanding recyclability in the cycling experiment, which was expected to become an efficient, economical, and environmentally friendly fluorescent sensing material. Furthermore, the detection mechanism of UA by the complex was explained in detail.

Europium(III) nitrate hexahydrate {Eu(NO3)3·6H2O}were purchased from Aladdin Ltd. Pyromellitic acid (H4btec), nicotinic acid (Hntca) and N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) were received from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Ltd. All the reagents and solvents were purchased from commercial sources and used without further treatments.

Elemental analyses for C, H and N were performed on the Vario-EL iii element analyzer. FT-IR spectra were obtained on a Nicolet Avatar 360 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer in the 4000–400 cm−1 region. PXRD pattern was collected on Ultima IV X-ray diffractometer with Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 0.15418 Å) at room temperature. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was carried out by using a Perkin-Elmer TGA-8000 analyzer in N2 atmosphere with a heating rate of 10 °C min−1. The solid-state fluorescence properties were measured at room temperature on an F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer. UV–vis spectra were recorded with a UV–2600 spectrophotometer in the range of 200–650 nm at room temperature. The luminescence lifetime measurements were performed on an FLS980 fluorescence spectrophotometer.

A mixture of Eu(NO3)3·6H2O (0.2 mmol, 89.2 mg), pyromellitic acid (0.2 mmol, 50.8 mg) and nicotinic acid (0.2 mmol, 24.6 mg) was dissolved in 12 mL distilled water at room temperature. The pH value of the solution was adjusted to neutral with 1.0 M NaOH. Then, the mixture was sealed in a 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and reacted at 120 ℃ for 5 days. After cooled to room temperature at a rate of 10 ℃ h−1, the colorless needle-like crystals were obtained by filtration and washed with distilled water. The yield was 54% based on the Eu salt. Elemental analysis (%): Calcd for C16H16EuNO14: C, 32.09; H, 2.67; N, 2.34; Found: C, 32.05; H, 2.73; N, 2.36. IR data (KBr, cm−1): 3076 w, 1648 m, 1566 m, 1397 s, 1219 m, 1124 m, 741 m, 622 s.

Single-crystal X-ray diffraction data for complex was collected on a Bruker D8-Quest diffractometer with graphite-monochromatic Mo Kα (λ = 0.71073 Å) at 293 K, an empirical absorption correction was carried out using the SADABS program [25]. The structures were solved by direct methods with SHELXS-97 and refined by full-matrix least squares techniques on F2 with SHELXL-97. All non-hydrogen atoms were refined anisotropically [26,27]. The hydrogen atoms of organic ligands were localized in their calculated positions and refined using a riding model. Details of X-ray experiment and crystal data were summarized in Table S1 and S2. The hydrogen bond information was shown in Table S3. The single crystal data have been recorded by the Cambridge Crystallographic Data Center, CCDC number of complex is 2046024.

Single X-ray diffraction analyses reveal that complex crystallizes in a triclinic system with the Pī space group. Each asymmetric unit contains one crystallographically independent Eu3+ ion, one pyromellitic acid (Hbtec3-) ligand, one nicotinic acid (Hntca) ligand, three coordinated water molecules and one free water molecule (Fig. 1 a). The pyridine N atom was further protonated to eliminate the negative charge carried by the deprotonated carboxyl group, which endowed the nicotinic acid (Hntca) ligand with electrically neutral. The charge remained conserved in the asymmetric unit of the complex. Each of Eu3+ is eight-coordinated by four carboxylate oxygen atoms (O3, O4, O9 and O10) from Hbtec3- ligands, one oxygen atom (O1) from one monodentate bridged nicotinic acid ligand, and the other three oxygen atoms were all derived from water molecules (O11, O12 and O13). The ranges of EuO bond lengths and OEuO bond angles were from 2.339(2) to 2.517(2) Å and from 51.70(6)° to 160.27(7)°, respectively, which are in the normal ranges of those observed in previously reported Eu3+ complexes. In addition, the metal ions formed dodecahedron with the surrounding coordination atoms. As shown in Fig. 1b, the deprotonated carboxyl groups of the Hbtec3- ligands had two coordination modes: one involved the carboxyl oxygen atoms coordinating with central metal ions through bidentate chelation and the other involved the carboxyl oxygen atoms bridging two metal ions (Symmetry code: A: -x, 1-y, -z; B: 1-x, 1-y, -z). The Eu3+ and Hbtec3- ligand were connected by coordination bonds in the a-axis direction to form infinitely extended one-dimensional double-chains (Fig. 1c). In addition to the coordination bonds, there were a variety of hydrogen bonds in other directions of the complex in Fig. 1d. The adjacent double-chains extended to a two-dimensional plane by four types of hydrogen bonding along the b-axis (N1-H1···O4 = 152.9°, H1···O4 = 2.06 Å; N1-H1···O3 = 130.1°, H1···O3 = 2.36 Å; O11-H11B···O5 = 136.2°, H11B···O5 = 2.33 Å; O12-H12B···O8 = 156.7°, H12B···O8 = 1.84 Å) (Fig. 1e). Furthermore, there were three kinds of hydrogen bonds in the c-axis direction (O13-H13A···O14 = 160.4°, H13A···O14 = 1.86 Å; O6-H6···O7 = 162.5°, H6···O7 = 1.82 Å; C4-H4···O14 = 146.8°, H4···O14 = 2.33 Å) (Fig. 1f), and the two-dimensional plane was further connected by the hydrogen bonds to form a three-dimensional supramolecular architecture (Fig. 2 a). Among them, the weak hydrogen bond CH···O was generated by the interaction between the H atom on the pyridine ring and the free water molecule, and the distance was 3.153 Å.

The three-dimensional supramolecular structure was simplified by ToposPro software [28]. As shown in Fig. 2b, Eu3+ was regarded as 6-connected nodes, and nicotinic acid, free water molecules and pyromellitic acid were regarded as 3, 4 and 8 connected nodes respectively. On this basis, these nodes further interlaced with each other to form a three-dimensional topological network. Point symbol could be represented as (32.4.52.6) (32.45.510.67.73.8) (32.45.55.63) (4.52) [29,30]. Among them, (32.4.52.6), (32.45.510.67.73.8), (32.45.55.63) and (4.52) correspond to free water molecules, H4btec, Eu3+ ions and Hntca, respectively.

As shown in Fig. S1, the absorption peaks at 1698 and 1294 cm−1 were caused by the CO and C-O stretching vibrations in the Hntca ligand, respectively. The peaks at 1693 and 1258 cm−1 were assigned to the CO and C-O stretching vibrations in the H4btec ligand, respectively [31]. In the IR spectra of the complex, the characteristic absorption band at 3076 cm−1 was attributed to the OH stretching vibration (νO-H) in the coordinated water molecules, which was lower than the common values. The phenomenon was due to the strong coordination interaction between the coordination water molecule and the metal ion (Eu3+), that was, the short coordination bond, which enhanced the inducing effect of Eu3+ on the electron cloud of the O-H bond, resulting in the weakening of the OH bond. [32,33]. Therefore, the stretching vibration frequency of O-H in the coordination water molecule shifted to low frequency. Furthermore, the CO stretching vibration (νC=O) in carboxyl groups was located at 1648 cm−1. The peak at 1566 cm−1 was caused by the CN stretching vibration (νC=N) of the pyridine ring in Hntca. The strong absorption band at 1397 cm−1 was attributed to the CC stretching vibration (νC=C) inside the benzene ring, and the CO stretching vibration (νC-O) in the carboxylic acid appeared at 1219 cm−1 [34]. Compared with the two ligands, the CO and CO characteristic peaks of the complex showed obvious red-shift, indicating that both ligands coordinated with metal ions [35,36], which was consistent with the results of the single-crystal experiments.

To identify the relative purity of the complex, the PXRD pattern of the complex was studied. The results showed that the PXRD pattern of the product was consistent with the data simulated from the single crystal structure, which confirms that the product obtained by the reaction had high phase purity. In practical application, many complexes are difficult to maintain the stability of the framework in aqueous solution, especially in acidic and alkaline environment. This limits their application fields to a great extent. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the PXRD pattern of the sample showed that it could maintain its original structure after soaking in aqueous solution with different pH values for 24 h (pH was adjusted by 1 mol/L HCl and 1 mol/L NaOH), which proved that it had excellent stability in water.

The TGA curve of the complex was shown in Fig. 3b, the complex has two main weight loss steps in the temperature range from 25 to 800 ℃ . The first lost weight between 140 ℃ and 245 ℃ was attributed to the loss of three coordination water molecules and one free water molecule, with a weight loss of 11.92% (calcd.12.03%). The second lost weight occurred at 341 ℃ and decreased continuously with the increase of temperature, which was caused by the decomposition of organic ligands. Finally, the framework of the complex collapsed completely at 628 ℃ . The above analysis results showed that the complex can exist stably before 341 ℃ , showing remarkable thermal stability.

In order to explore the luminescence properties of the complex, the solid-state luminescence spectra of dual-ligand and complex were measured at ambient temperature. As shown in Fig. S2, the H4btec exhibited a narrow emission peak at 380 nm under the excitation wavelength of 303 nm. The emission spectrum showed a wide band at 504 nm under the excitation at 439 nm, which was caused by the π-π* electron transition in the Hntca ligand. Moreover, in the fluorescence spectrum of the complex, the characteristic emission peak of Eu3+ at 593, 613 and 692 nm was observed when excited by 396 nm UV light, which belong to 5D0→7F1, 5D0→7F2 and 5D0→7F4 transitions, respectively. At the same time, a weak and wide emission band appeared near 500 nm, which may be attributed to the H4btec molecule (Fig. 4 a). The decay curve is shown in Fig. S3. For the complex emission band centered at 593 nm, the best fit for the decay curve of the fluorescence lifetime was τ = 3.324 ns.

Furthermore, the fluorescence behavior of the complex was further investigated in detail by quantum-chemical simulation. The triplet energy level (T1) of H4btec and Hntca were calculated using Gaussian 09 D.01 Linux software with M06-2X functional, which were 22,150 and 20168 cm−1, respectively. The triplet energy of Hntca was close to the 5D0 vibrational energy level of Eu3+ ion (17250 cm−1), indicating that it could be used as an “antenna molecule” to sensitize Eu3+ luminescence. On the contrary, there was a large energy level difference between the T1 triplet energy level of H4btec and Eu3+, which makes it difficult to realize effective energy transfer, so most of the absorbed excitation light energy was used to excite its own electrons, which correspond to the wide emission band near 500 nm in Fig. 4a. In summary, the luminescence mechanism of the complex was accurately and reasonably explained by the fluorescence measurement results and the combination of quantum computation. Fig. 4b shows the energy transfer process between Eu3+ and the two ligands.

Based on the novel supramolecular structure, excellent luminescence properties, high thermal and water stability of the complex, it was necessary to explore whether the complex could be used as an efficient and sensitive fluorescence sensor. Therefore, the fluorescence response of the complex to various organic molecules was investigated. In the experiment, the sample (2 mg) was fully ground and dispersed in 5 mL of aqueous solution to form a suspension. Subsequently, uric acid (UA), urea, glutamic acid (Glu), aspartic acid (Asp), ascorbic acid (AA), glucose, guanine (Gua), and adenine (Adn) were added into the suspension, and the fluorescence spectra were recorded 20 min of ultrasound. The experimental results showed that the luminescence properties of the complex were influenced by organic molecules, and uric acid had a considerable influence on the luminescence intensities of the complex, sharply quenching luminescence intensities. In contrast, other substances had no significant effect on the luminescence response (Fig. 5 a).

In order to evaluate the sensitivity of the complex to the detection of uric acid molecules, the fluorescence spectra of the complex aqueous solution containing different concentrations of uric acid molecules were measured. As shown in Fig. 5b, under the excitation of UV light at 396 nm, the characteristic fluorescence emission intensity at 593 nm (5D0→7F1), 613 nm (5D0→7F2) and 692 nm (5D0→7F4) decreased as the amount of uric acid increased, and the emission band at 589 nm showed the most significant change. This quenching effect was quantitatively rationalized using the Stern-Volmer equation: (I0/I) = Ksv [A] + 1, where I0 is the fluorescence intensity before addition of uric acid, I is the fluorescence intensity after addition of uric acid, [A] is the concentration of the uric acid, and Ksv is the quenching coefficient [37]. As shown in Fig. 5c, The Stern-Volmer curve showed that there was a good linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and the concentration of uric acid in the range of 0–100 μM (R2 = 0.9895), and KSV was calculated as 3.94 × 104 M−1. Furthermore, the detection limit (LOD) of the uric acid was found to be 1.2 μM, calculated by the equation of 3σ/K (where σ is the value of the slope and K is the value of KSV), which was much lower than the normal concentration of uric acid in serum (0.13–0.46 mM) and urine (1.49–4.50 mM) of healthy human [38]. The complex was compared with other previously reported fluorescence sensors for detecting uric acid (Table S4) [39–45], indicating that it was a promising and highly sensitive sensor material. Further, we measured the fluorescence intensity of complex after adding different potential interferences including L-serine (L-Ser), L-cysteine (L-Cys), Sucrose (SUC), dopamine hydrochloride (DA), creatinine (Cre), hippuric acid (HA), Glutathione (GSH), Na+, and K+ in the absence and presence of UA. As shown in Fig. 5d, a variety of interferences have no significant effect on the fluorescence properties of the complex, indicating that it has excellent anti-infection ability in detecting of uric acid.

In addition to detection sensitivity and selectivity, recyclability is also an important index to measure the chemical sensors. Good recyclability is conducive to the realization of efficient, economical, environmentally-friendly and low-cost substance detection in practical applications [46,47]. Thus, the reusability of the complex was investigated. 20 mg of the sample was dispersed into the UA suspension. After centrifugation, the sample was washed with distilled water and dried in the vacuum. The solid product was collected for fluorescence test. The recycling experiments showed that the quenching efficiency of the complex remained basically unchanged after 5 cycles, indicating that the complex had remarkable recyclability in the detection of the UA molecules (Fig. S4).

The fluorescence quenching mechanism of UA on the complex was studied by using PXRD and UV–Vis spectra combined with density functional theory (DFT). As shown in Fig. S5, the XRD pattern of the complex basically remained unchanged after the detection of UA, indicating that the structural change of the complex was not the cause of the quenching process. There was a large overlap between the fluorescence excitation spectra of the complex and the absorption spectra of UA (Fig. S6), which indicated that UA competed with the ligand to absorb the energy of the excitation light, resulting in a decrease in the energy transferred to Eu3+ by ligand as “antenna molecules”, thus leading to fluorescence quenching [48].

The photoinduced electron transfer (PET) process also affects the fluorescence properties of the system. The geometry optimization and orbital diagrams of nicotinic acid and uric acid were calculated via the DFT method. The DFT calculations were performed using the Dmol3 module in Material Studio software with the GGA/PW91 functional and DNP basis set (Fig. 6 ). The calculated results showed that the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of UA was lower than that of the nicotinic acid ligand. The excited electrons in the ligand were more inclined to transfer to the LUMO level of the UA molecule, and the absorbed energy was released in a non-radiative form when the electrons returned to the ground state, which greatly affected the energy transfer from the ligand to the 5D0 vibrational energy level of Eu3+, leading to the quenching of the characteristic fluorescence of Eu3+. Based on the above analysis, the fluorescence quenching process was caused by energy absorption competition and photoinduced electron transfer.

In summary, a novel complex with good thermal and water stability was successfully prepared. The infinitely extended three-dimensional network was formed through the coordination bond and hydrogen bond, which possessed a novel and rare topological structure. The quantum chemistry calculation result showed that the Hntca ligand served as an “antenna molecule” to transfer the absorbed energy to Eu3+, thus exhibiting the characteristic spectrum of Eu3+. The complex realized the selective detection of UA by fluorescence quenching response in the presence of various interferences, and showed high detection sensitivity and remarkable recoverability. On this basis, the detection mechanism was investigated in detail by several characterization methods combined with DFT. The competition of UA for the absorption of excitation light energy was the dominant factor that hindered the energy transfer from ligand to Eu3+. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of the system was further quenched by PET from ligands to UA molecules.

Xiao-Yang Zhao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Qi-Shan Yang: Investigation, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Jia Wang: Software, Investigation, Formal analysis, Validation. Dong-Lei Fu: Software, Validation. Dao-Kuan Jiang: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Inner Mongolia Natural Science Foundation (No. 2016ZD05).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.120065.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrostatically mediated layer-by-layer assembly of nitrogen-doped graphene/PVDDA/gold nanoparticle composites for electrochemical detection of uric acid

Li, Electrostatically mediated layer-by-layer assembly of nitrogen-doped graphene/PVDDA/gold nanoparticle composites for electrochemical detection of uric acid, Anal. Bioanal. Chem. 412 (2020) 669-680.

Hu, Fluorescent detection of uric acid through photoinduced electron transfer using luminol-terbium(III) nanoparticles synthesized via aggregation-induced fluorescence strategy, Dyes Pigm. 172 (2020) 107797.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A luminescent terbium metal-organic framework for highly sensitive and selective detection of uric acid in aqueous media

Bioelectron. 91 (2017) 734-740.
